text: Cognitive capital — the aggregate capacity of a workforce to think clearly and at depth — is not measured on any balance sheet, yet economists increasingly recognize it as a primary driver of organizational productivity. A study by McKinsey Global Institute estimated that high-skill knowledge workers spend only 39 percent of their time on role-specific tasks, with the remainder consumed by email, meetings, and administrative coordination. The implication is striking: organizations that protect their employees' focused time are not offering a perk — they are recovering a resource that chronic distraction has been systematically destroying.
question: What does the McKinsey study cited in the passage reveal about knowledge worker time allocation?

options.text: Knowledge workers spend 61 percent of their time on role-specific, high-value tasks
Email alone accounts for more than half of a typical knowledge worker's day
Only 39 percent of knowledge worker time is spent on role-specific tasks
Meeting frequency is the single largest predictor of cognitive capital loss
